_comment: EXAMPLE MANIFEST — the filled-in GTG course manifest for reference.
_see: Rename/copy _course_manifest.json (the blank template) and fill it in for a new course.

course.title: Island Mode Operation: Design Principles for GTG Packaging Engineers

intro.subtitle: Design Principles for Gas Turbine Generator Packaging Engineers

screens.label: Title Card
vo: Welcome to this course on island mode operation for gas turbine generator systems...

screens.label: Who This Course Is For
vo: Whether your role is in engineering, technical sales, or customer support...

screens.label: Course Application
vo: Throughout this course, our reference application is a data centre powered entirely by multiple, paralleled gas turbine generators.

screens.label: Learning Objectives
vo: By the end of this course, you will be able to achieve the following six learning objectives.
One. Explain why island mode requires different operating limits than grid-parallel operation.
Two. Define the island mode derate and the incremental reserve margin...
Three. Describe the governor and excitation control functions...
Five. Explain how Dry Low Emissions combustion constraints affect step-load limits.
Six. Describe the coordination requirements for multi-unit parallel operation.
These objectives reflect the knowledge needed to...

objectives.text: Explain why island mode requires different operating limits than grid-parallel operation.
Define the island mode derate and the incremental reserve margin, and apply them to a representative 18 MW class turbine system.
Describe the governor and excitation control functions required for stable island mode operation.
Identify the protection systems — UFLS, ROCOF, and excitation limiting — and explain their coordination.
Explain how Dry Low Emissions combustion constraints affect step-load limits.
Describe the coordination requirements for multi-unit parallel operation in a data centre power plant.

int2_cards.body: This course supports engineers, technical sales staff, and support personnel involved with gas turbine generator packages.
Applications focus on critical, grid-independent power systems — including large-scale data centres.
Understanding island mode enables sound design decisions and effective communication across project teams and with customers.

int3_kp.body:
Application focus:
Multi-unit paralleled GTG power systems for data centres.

Supplier examples used:
Kawasaki Heavy Industries (KHI) turbines · Jeumont Electric alternators
Principles apply broadly across the industry.

intro.int3_highlight:
⚡ No utility grid connection —
the GTGs are the grid.

int3_closing.body: Every load change, every motor start, every fault — lands entirely on the packaged GTG system. There is nothing else.

screens.label: Section Intro
screens.vo: Section one. In this section we establish the fundamental difference between grid-connected and island mode operation...

screens.label: The 2-Step Load Event
screens.vo: Every load connection event in island mode is actually two steps...

quiz.quiz_vo: You have reached the end of Section one. Complete the four-question quiz...
quiz.fail_msg: Review the section material and try again.

pool.stem: What is the primary reason a gas turbine generator operating in island mode must be derated?

options.text: The alternator produces less power without reactive grid support
The governor requires headroom above the operating point to respond to sudden load increases
DLE combustion systems require additional fuel at full load
Grid connection provides additional cooling to the turbine exhaust

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. The island mode derate creates governor headroom for transient response — it is a control margin, not a thermal limit.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. The derate creates governor headroom. It is a control margin, not a thermal or electrical limit.

exam.pass_msg: You have achieved a passing score and will receive course completion credit for KIR-GTG-001.
exam.fail_msg: A score of 80% or higher is required. Please review the sections indicated and arrange a retake.
section_names: Section 2 — Power Ratings, Derating, and Reserve Margin
Section 3 — Governor and Excitation Control Systems
Section 4 — Protection Strategies: UFLS, ROCOF, and Limiting
Section 5 — Combustion System Constraints and DLE
Section 6 — Multi-Unit Parallel Operation in Data Centres

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly explains why the island mode derate for an 18 MW class gas turbine is approximately 10%?
options: The turbine combustion system cannot sustain full firing temperature without grid support.
The derate accounts for elevated ambient temperatures in data centre environments.
The derate is a control margin for transient response, reactive power, and combustion stability — not a thermal limit.
Alternators cannot produce rated MVA without grid reactive support.
questions.feedback: The island mode derate is a control margin. The governor needs headroom above the operating point to respond to sudden load steps. The ceiling for an 18 MW turbine is approximately 16.2 MW.
